Five ways Boho can help
Name the problem before choosing the service.

Websites, search, analytics, migration, research, and automation are tools. Boho chooses the smallest useful combination after understanding the business, the customer, the market, and the failure in front of us.
Work and evidence
See the work, the method, and the limits of the evidence.

Boho does not publish invented client results. Evidence is labeled by source: owned property, public repository or tool, sample document, synthetic demonstration, fictional concept interface, or client work published with permission.

Ownership should be visible
A website is more than its page files.
The domain, registrar, DNS, hosting, content system, forms, analytics, search accounts, email connections, repositories, and integrations may be controlled in different places.
Boho maps those dependencies before a migration or rescue so the business can see what it owns, what a provider controls, what remains unknown, and what must be recovered or replaced.

What happens after contact
A clear next step, not an immediate sales ambush.
The exact path depends on the problem, but the decision sequence should remain understandable.
1. Send the situation

Share the website or system, the business context, what is happening, and what matters most. Do not send passwords, private keys, recovery codes, payment-card information, medical information, or other secrets.
2. Initial review
Boho reviews the visible information and normally replies to a standard inquiry within two business days.
3. Written recommendation
The reply may ask for clarification, recommend a smaller first step, identify the likely service, or explain that the project is not a fit.
4. Scope and price
Paid work begins only after the deliverables, exclusions, responsibilities, starting condition, price, payment terms, and planning range are written down.
5. Implementation
Boho completes the approved work and records material changes. New direction, access, cost, or risk requires approval rather than appearing later as an unexplained surprise.
6. Verification
The agreed pages, forms, redirects, events, integrations, deployments, or records are checked. External systems may take additional time to process or respond.
7. Owner documentation
The business receives the records, ownership information, known limitations, and follow-up checks stated in the scope.
Design changes with the business
One visual system should not force every company into one personality.
A contractor, professional service, hospitality business, and online retailer ask different things of design because their customers, proof, service areas, inventory, and next actions are different.
Fictional concept interface — not client work

Retail or hospitality
Visual identity, location details, hours, discovery, reservations, and visits should work together.
Online retailer
Product hierarchy, category discovery, shipping information, trust, and focused product pages should support purchase decisions.
The operating standard
Own the system. Understand the work. Measure what informs a decision.
No hidden ownership
No fabricated proof
No ranking guarantees
No unexplained retainers
No unnecessary rebuild

Pricing & Scope Guide
Starting prices without the agency guessing game.

These are public planning prices, not automatic quotes. Final scope depends on the current system, the condition of the assets, content readiness, third-party dependencies, urgency, and the amount of uncertainty that must be removed before implementation.
A starting price is useful only when it says what starts there. Each minimum below describes the smallest complete scope Boho is prepared to sell responsibly.
Send the Situation
/start/
Compare the Five Services
/services/
What a starting price means

Starting prices apply to the smallest complete scope described. Final price depends on the current system, access, content readiness, integrations, third-party dependencies, urgency, risk, and the amount of uncertainty that must be removed before implementation.
The written proposal controls the final deliverables, exclusions, responsibilities, payment terms, planning range, ownership, support boundaries, and third-party costs.

Boho does not guarantee rankings, traffic, leads, sales, platform approval, or a specific business result. The work is scoped around documented improvements, measurable signals, and decisions supported by the evidence available.

Timelines are planning ranges, not guarantees. Work begins after the required access, content, approvals, and initial payment are available. Provider delays, platform reviews, missing assets, scope changes, and client response time can extend the calendar.
Boho service starting prices
Initial public review — Free
A bounded review of public information intended to identify the likely problem, the most relevant service category, and the next useful discussion.
It is not a complete audit, private-account review, provider recovery engagement, implementation project, quote, or guarantee of availability.
A straightforward free public-information review is normally completed within two business days after the required website and business context are received.
#initial-review
SEO reporting — Starting at $95 per month

A small reporting scope for one straightforward website or property, a limited set of supported sources, one primary customer-action path, analyst review, prioritized findings, limitations, and a written decision record.

The minimum reporting scope does not include website changes, content production, technical repair, custom tracking implementation, hosting, managed email, or a standing meeting unless the proposal says otherwise.
Ongoing work: organized in monthly cycles with a named priority, completed-work record, verification, limitations, and next decision.
#ongoing-seo
SEO implementation — Starting at $450 per month

A narrow recurring implementation scope for one website, one primary market, monthly review, one defined implementation priority, basic technical and search monitoring, and a written completed-work record.
The minimum retainer is not unlimited access to every SEO, website, design, content, local, analytics, or consulting service Boho offers.
First implementation cycle: usually begins within 2–4 weeks after scope approval, depending on access, technical condition, content readiness, and the amount of work included.
#ongoing-seo
Focused website improvement — Starting at $750

One bounded improvement project on a sound existing website, such as a high-value page, customer path, form, information-architecture problem, performance issue, accessibility issue, or conversion obstacle.
This price does not cover a complete redesign, new website, large content program, broad platform migration, or unlimited revisions.
Initial recommendation and scope: usually 2–5 business days after the current site, business requirements, and available assets are reviewed.
#websites
New website — Starting at $1,500

A small, complete website with a bounded page plan, responsive visual system, agreed core pages, contact path, search foundations, basic analytics readiness, launch verification, and owner-facing documentation.

The minimum does not include unlimited pages, custom applications, large content migration, complex ecommerce, specialized integrations, professional photography, regulated-industry review, or third-party costs unless stated.
Small or straightforward website: usually 4–8 weeks after required access, content decisions, assets, approvals, and initial payment are available.
#websites
Substantial redesign — Starting at $1,500
A bounded redesign of an existing small website when the structure, customer path, visual system, mobile experience, ownership, or search foundation requires material change.
A redesign does not automatically include migration of every page, platform, integration, account, form, or historical asset.
Substantial redesign or migration: usually 6–12 weeks. Large content inventories, custom integrations, provider dependencies, or delayed approvals may require more time.
#websites
Provider Rescue Assessment — Starting at $350

A bounded ownership, access, dependency, and migration-risk review intended to identify what the business controls, what a provider controls, what remains unknown, and what must happen before a safe move.
The assessment does not include unauthorized access, guaranteed recovery, legal representation, a complete migration, redesign, or emergency implementation.
Ownership and dependency inventory: usually 2–5 business days when the required information and authorized access are available.
#provider-rescue
Migration assistance — Starting at $1,000

A small, straightforward migration with confirmed authority and access, bounded content or platform scope, preservation planning, required redirect work, launch checks, and owner-facing documentation.
The minimum does not cover unresolved ownership disputes, large content inventories, complex email systems, broad custom integrations, legal recovery, or a complete redesign unless stated.

#provider-rescue
Focused audit or strategy — Starting at $350

The minimum does not include unlimited properties, competitors, data sources, implementation, certification, legal opinion, security guarantee, appraisal, or specialist approval.

#research-audits
Custom discovery — Starting at $500

A bounded discovery process for one workflow or tool concept, including users, inputs, outputs, decision rules, human-review points, dependencies, risks, build-versus-buy analysis, and an implementation recommendation.
Discovery does not include a finished production system unless the proposal expressly includes implementation.
Paid discovery, when required: usually 1–2 weeks.

What usually changes the price
Price increases when the work becomes larger, less certain, more urgent, more integrated, or more exposed to third-party risk.
More websites, properties, pages, templates, services, products, locations, markets, or languages
Large or disorganized content inventories
Missing ownership, access, documentation, or reliable backups
Custom forms, integrations, APIs, dashboards, ecommerce, or data migration
Specialized accessibility, security, privacy, compliance, legal, medical, financial, or regulated-industry review
Provider disputes, account recovery, DNS dependencies, or platform approval
Professional photography, illustration, video, copy development, or brand work beyond the stated scope
Priority scheduling, emergency triage, or work outside ordinary support boundaries
More meetings, review rounds, stakeholders, or approval dependencies
Third-party software, hosting, email, transaction, licensing, data, or infrastructure costs
Assessment credit

An eligible paid assessment is credited in full toward a larger engagement in the same service category when the later work is approved within the period stated in the proposal. The proposal controls eligibility, timing, and exclusions.

The credit has no cash value and does not apply to unrelated work, third-party costs, recurring reporting already delivered, hosting, or priority premiums unless the proposal expressly says otherwise.
Managed hosting and defined email services

Standard managed hosting may be included at no separate hosting charge for an eligible website while the qualifying service relationship remains active and in good standing. Eligibility, platform limits, third-party costs, support boundaries, ownership, and exit handling are defined in the project agreement. This is not a promise of unlimited infrastructure or unlimited support.

Reporting-only plans do not qualify for included infrastructure. Any email service must be named and defined in the proposal, including whether it covers routing, aliases, transactional messages, stored mailboxes, sending limits, retention, renewal, or another bounded service.
What happens before paid work begins

Boho provides a written scope or proposal stating the deliverables, exclusions, client responsibilities, dependencies, price, payment schedule, planning range, ownership, support boundaries, and third-party costs.
A public price, submitted form, email, conversation, estimate, or preliminary recommendation does not create a client relationship or require either party to proceed.

Source class and limitations must remain visible on the artifact itself. They may not be hidden behind hover, a tooltip, an accordion, or a general disclaimer elsewhere on the page.
A website, tool, dataset, system, or experiment owned and operated by Boho or its operating company.
An owned property demonstrates Boho’s direct work and operating method. It is not client work and does not establish that another business will receive the same result.
Source code, documentation, or a functioning public utility that can be inspected directly.
A public repository or tool demonstrates implementation and documentation. It does not prove production suitability for every business, environment, or scale.
A realistic example of a report, map, checklist, proposal structure, handoff record, or other client-facing artifact.
A sample document demonstrates format and method. It is not evidence that the sample was delivered to a client or produced the example outcome.
A demonstration built from invented, public-safe, or deliberately artificial data to show how a system behaves.
A synthetic demonstration shows mechanics and presentation. It is not a real client dataset or commercial result.
A deliberately fictional design used to demonstrate visual range or information architecture.
A fictional concept interface is not client work, a launched business, a testimonial, or a claimed result.
Work completed for a real client and published only with explicit permission and accurate context.
Client work must state the scope, time period, evidence, limitations, and factors outside Boho’s control. One client result is not a guarantee for another business.

How Boho records work
A useful record shows the problem, the change, the evidence, and the uncertainty.
Context — What system, business problem, and constraint was being addressed?
Intervention — What was changed, built, measured, migrated, or deliberately left alone?
Validation — How was the change checked?
Result — What was directly observed after the work?
Limitation — What cannot be concluded from the available evidence?
Next decision — What should happen next, and what would cause the recommendation to change?
Boho will not manufacture proof to fill an empty portfolio slot.

When real client evidence becomes publishable, it will be added with permission, accurate dates, scope, method, limitations, and supported results. Until then, owned properties, public systems, sample documents, technical records, and clearly fictional concepts remain the honest evidence available.
See enough to decide whether the method fits your problem.

Timelines are planning ranges, not guarantees. Work begins after the required access, content, approvals, and initial payment are available. Provider delays, platform reviews, missing assets, scope changes, and client response time can extend the calendar.
Choose the engagement shape
Not every problem deserves a retainer.
One-time work
A focused improvement, website project, migration, rescue, audit, strategy review, discovery process, or custom build should end when the approved scope is complete and verified.
Recurring work

A recurring engagement makes sense only when the next cycle has a useful purpose, such as continued search improvement, reporting, website maintenance, content development, measurement, or another named operational need.
When recurring work should stop

Boho should recommend reducing, pausing, or ending recurring work when the agreed objectives are substantially complete, the remaining work no longer justifies the cost, a focused project would be more economical, useful measurement cannot be established, or required access and cooperation are unavailable.
A retainer is not a membership fee for being allowed to ask what happened.
The service menu is not the strategy
The recommendation begins with the business and the evidence.
What does the business sell or operate?
Which customers, projects, products, services, locations, or workflows matter most?
What is the visible failure?
Which accounts, platforms, providers, data, and dependencies are involved?
What evidence exists, and what remains uncertain?
Which change is most likely to improve the decision or system?
Which work can be deferred, avoided, or handled more cheaply?

Boho uses the smallest useful combination of services capable of addressing the real problem. Material changes in direction, cost, access, or risk require approval rather than appearing later as an unexplained surprise.
Initial public review
Useful orientation before a larger scope.
A bounded review of public information can help identify the likely problem, the most relevant service category, and the next useful discussion.
It is not a complete audit, private-account review, provider recovery engagement, implementation project, quote, or guarantee of availability.
Initial public review — Free
A straightforward free public-information review is normally completed within two business days after the required website and business context are received.

Fictional concept interface — not client work
Fictional home improvement website concept
A concept emphasizing project evidence, service-area clarity, credentials, estimates, and urgent contact paths.

A deliberately fictional responsive website concept for a home improvement service. The layout emphasizes completed-project imagery, service areas, credentials, estimate requests, and urgent contact information. It is not client work or a performance claim.

A deliberately fictional responsive website concept for a local professional service. The layout emphasizes calm service navigation, practitioner or team information, local proof, and an appointment path. It is not client work or a performance claim.

A concept emphasizing visual identity, location details, hours, discovery, reservations, and visits.

A deliberately fictional responsive website concept for a retail or hospitality business. The layout emphasizes visual identity, location details, hours, discovery, and a reservation or visit action. It is not client work or a performance claim.

From local discovery to a qualified action
Search or map discovery
Relevant service or location page

Call, form, booking, purchase, or visit

The objective is not the largest possible traffic number. It is a clearer path from relevant discovery to a qualified action the business can support.

A six-stage path starts with search or map discovery, moves to a relevant service or location page, accurate offer and trust information, a call form booking purchase or visit, qualified-action evidence, and the next monthly priority.
A practical measurement hierarchy
Collection is working
The right pages and actions are visible
Relevant people are finding the business
Qualified actions are increasing or improving
The business can make a better decision
A high-level result is not credible when the collection, page identity, action tracking, or business context underneath it is unreliable.
A five-level hierarchy begins with working data collection, then visibility into the right pages and actions, relevant discovery, qualified actions, and finally a better business decision.
The monthly operating cycle
Review the current system
Name the priority
Complete the approved work
Verify what changed
Document the cycle
A monthly plan should continue because the next cycle has a useful purpose, not because cancellation has been made inconvenient.
A five-stage repeating cycle reviews the current system, names one priority, completes the approved work, verifies what changed, and documents the cycle before beginning again.
A website should support the customer decision
What does the business do?
Is this relevant to me?
Why should I trust it?
What does it cost or require?
What should I do next?
A page can be visually distinctive without making the visitor excavate the offer, proof, price, or next step.
A five-question customer journey asks what the business does, whether it is relevant, why it should be trusted, what it costs or requires, and what the visitor should do next.

Exact offer
Who it is for
Starting price and planning range
Scope and exclusions
Evidence
Process
Next action
The page answers the buyer’s practical questions before asking for contact.
A commercial page is divided into seven regions: exact offer, intended buyer, starting price and planning range, scope and exclusions, evidence, process, and next action.
Historical URL and redirect map
Preserve
Redirect to closest verified replacement
Consolidate with review
Remove intentionally
Unresolved — do not guess
A migration preserves useful entry points by making a specific decision for every known historical URL.
A table maps historical URLs to one of five decisions: preserve, redirect to the closest verified replacement, consolidate with review, remove intentionally, or leave unresolved without guessing.
A provider rescue in six documented stages
Inventory
Confirm authority and ownership
Preserve useful assets
Plan the move
Launch and verify
Document the new system
The objective is not merely to move files. It is to recover control, preserve value, reduce risk, and leave clearer records behind.
A six-stage provider-rescue sequence inventories the system, confirms authority and ownership, preserves useful assets, plans the move, launches and verifies the result, and documents the new system.
How findings become priorities
Difficulty and dependency
Likely impact and risk
Do first
Plan carefully
Useful when capacity allows
Do not spend on this yet
A finding is not a priority until impact, evidence, difficulty, dependency, and cost are considered together.

A two-axis matrix compares likely impact and risk against difficulty and dependency. Findings fall into four groups: do first, plan carefully, useful when capacity allows, or do not spend on this yet.
From evidence to a decision
Context and question
Sources and data quality
Observed findings
Reasonable inference
Priority and recommendation
Limitation and validation plan
Observation, inference, and recommendation remain separate so uncertainty cannot quietly become a fact.

A six-stage evidence sequence moves from context and question through sources and data quality, observed findings, reasonable inference, priority and recommendation, and finally limitation and validation plan.
A repeated workflow before and after a focused tool
Manual intake
Repeated copying
Unclear ownership
Hidden exceptions
No reliable record
Structured input
Defined decision rules
Human review where required
Recorded output
Visible failure state
Automation should make a sound process clearer and more reliable, not make an unclear process fail faster.

A before-and-after comparison shows a manual workflow with repeated copying, unclear ownership, hidden exceptions, and no reliable record. The improved workflow uses structured input, defined rules, required human review, recorded output, and visible failure states.
Should this become custom software?
Is the workflow understood?
Is the action authorized and safe?
Does existing software solve it economically?
Can correct output and exceptions be defined?
Does the expected value justify discovery, testing, and maintenance?
Clarify the process first
Do not automate this
Use existing software
Run bounded discovery
Consider a focused custom build
Custom code is justified only after the process, authority, alternatives, correctness, and value are understood.

A five-question decision tree asks whether the workflow is understood, authorized and safe, already solved economically, capable of defined correct output and exceptions, and valuable enough to justify discovery testing and maintenance. The outcomes are clarify the process, do not automate, use existing software, run discovery, or consider a custom build.

Request bounded urgent triage for a broken website, bad launch, failed form, provider-access problem, redirect error, DNS issue, tracking loss, or migration failure.
Emergency Website Help
Something broke. Stop it from getting worse.

Use this page for a verified urgent website, launch, form, redirect, provider-access, DNS, tracking, or migration problem that is actively affecting customers, business operations, ownership, or the public site.

Emergency requests are triaged as capacity, authority, access, and risk controls allow. Submitting a request does not guarantee immediate response, acceptance, restoration, resolution, or a specific recovery time.
Describe the Emergency
#emergency-request
Use the Standard Project Inquiry
/start/
Urgency does not remove the need for authorized access, verification, documentation, or a rollback plan.
Use this route when
The problem is active, material, and time-sensitive.
The public website is unavailable or serving the wrong content.
A launch or migration broke important pages, forms, redirects, tracking, booking, checkout, or customer contact.
The domain or DNS changed unexpectedly or stopped resolving correctly.
A critical form, notification, booking, checkout, or inquiry path stopped working.
Important URLs now return errors, redirect incorrectly, or expose the wrong indexation controls.
Authorized access was lost during an active incident and business operations are being affected.
A provider change or account action created an immediate website, email, analytics, or customer-path dependency problem.
A known production change needs a bounded rollback or stabilization review.
Use the standard inquiry instead when
records.routeDestination: Use the standard inquiry instead when

records.exactValue: Send the Situation
/start/
What happens first
Triage before intervention.
1. Confirm the incident
Boho reviews the public failure, known change, affected customer path, timing, and the authority available to act.
2. Reduce avoidable damage
The first recommendation may be to stop further changes, preserve evidence, restore a known-good state, contact a provider, or prepare a rollback rather than begin a broad repair.
3. Define the bounded action
Paid work begins only after the immediate action, access, risk, price or billing basis, responsibility, and verification method are understood as clearly as the incident allows.
4. Verify and document
Boho checks the agreed recovery or stabilization target and records the action, known limitations, unresolved dependencies, and next recommended step.
Priority work is separately reviewed.
Emergency requests are accepted only when Boho has the capacity, authority, access, and risk controls required for the proposed action.

Priority, accelerated, after-hours, or work that displaces scheduled projects may cost more. The billing basis or minimum will be disclosed and approved before paid work begins when the circumstances allow.

Boho does not guarantee continuous availability, a particular response time, provider cooperation, account recovery, rollback success, restored rankings, restored data, or a specific resolution time unless a written engagement expressly states a binding commitment.
Boho uses authorized access and lawful recovery methods.
Boho does not bypass credentials or security controls.
Boho does not impersonate an owner or authorized representative.
Boho does not falsify authority or provider records.
Boho does not seize, disable, or alter accounts without authorization.
Boho does not guarantee cooperation from a former provider, registrar, platform, hosting company, mailbox provider, or search engine.
Some incidents require action by the owner, provider, platform, attorney, insurer, cybersecurity specialist, law-enforcement agency, or another responsible professional.
#emergency-request
